best_for: Server-rendered apps or same-origin SPAs where the backend sets the session and the browser handles it automatically
avoid_when: Cross-origin API consumed by a mobile app or third-party client, where cookies don't travel the same way
tradeoffs: Browser handles the token for you and HttpOnly mitigates XSS token theft, but requires CSRF protection and a server-side session store (or a signed/encrypted cookie)

best_for: Stateless APIs, mobile apps, or serverless/edge functions that shouldn't hold shared session state
avoid_when: Traditional server-rendered app where cookie sessions are simpler and CSRF protection is already in place
tradeoffs: Scales horizontally with no shared session store, but revoking a token before its natural expiry requires an extra mechanism (short expiry + refresh, or a revocation list)

best_for: Any JWT-based auth system — issue short-lived access tokens (minutes) plus a long-lived refresh token, rotating the refresh token on each use
avoid_when: Session-cookie-based auth where the session store itself already handles expiry/revocation server-side
tradeoffs: Limits the blast radius of a leaked access token (short life) while keeping the user logged in; rotation detects refresh-token theft (reuse of an old rotated token signals compromise)

best_for: Consumer apps prioritizing low-friction signup/login where users may not want to manage a password
avoid_when: High-frequency logins where waiting for an email each time is real friction — pair with a persistent session so it's a one-time cost
tradeoffs: Removes password-related support burden (resets, reuse, breaches) entirely, but login is only as fast as the user's email client and requires a reliable transactional email pipeline

option: One-Time Password (OTP) via SMS or Email
best_for: Markets/users where email delivery is unreliable but SMS is ubiquitous, or as a second factor alongside another primary method
avoid_when: SMS-only OTP as your *only* factor for high-value accounts — SIM-swapping makes SMS the weakest common MFA channel
tradeoffs: Familiar UX for many users, but SMS delivery costs money per message and is the least secure common MFA channel due to SIM-swap risk

best_for: Products wanting the strongest phishing-resistant auth with the least user friction on supporting devices/browsers
avoid_when: Users on older devices/browsers without WebAuthn support — always offer a fallback method
tradeoffs: Phishing-resistant by design (the credential is bound to the origin) and no shared secret to leak from a server breach, but requires a fallback path for unsupported devices and slightly higher implementation complexity than password/magic-link

best_for: Apps that need traditional password auth (enterprise expectation, offline-capable login) — use Argon2id specifically, not MD5/SHA1/bcrypt if starting fresh
avoid_when: A greenfield consumer app with no requirement for passwords at all — prefer passwordless (magic link/passkey) as the primary method instead
tradeoffs: Argon2id is the current best-practice hash (memory-hard, resistant to GPU cracking) — bcrypt is an acceptable fallback if your framework/library only supports that, but never roll your own hashing or use fast general-purpose hashes (MD5, SHA family) for passwords

option: Password Reset via Time-Limited Single-Use Token
best_for: Any system supporting password login — the reset token must expire (typically 15-60 min) and be invalidated after first use
avoid_when: Never applicable to skip — every password-based system needs this flow, and it's a common target for security review
tradeoffs: A stolen or replayed reset link should not work after expiry or after one use; always invalidate all existing sessions on successful password reset (DB039)

option: Invalidate All Sessions on Password Change
best_for: Every password-based auth system, triggered on password reset or explicit password change
avoid_when: Never skip — an attacker who compromised an old session should be logged out the moment the legitimate user changes their password
tradeoffs: Closes the window where a compromised session/token remains valid after the user has taken the corrective action of changing their password

option: Login Attempt Rate Limiting / Account Lockout
best_for: Every password-login endpoint — rate limit by IP and by account to blunt credential-stuffing and brute-force attacks
avoid_when: A rate-limit configuration so aggressive it locks out legitimate users on a shared IP (office/school NAT) — tune thresholds and prefer progressive delays over hard lockouts where possible
tradeoffs: Blunts automated credential-stuffing attacks, but must be paired with a clear unlock path (time-based or verified-email-based) so it doesn't become a denial-of-service vector against real users

best_for: The most broadly recommended second factor for account security — works offline, no per-use cost, widely supported (Google/Microsoft/Authy)
avoid_when: Users who will realistically never set up an authenticator app — always pair with a documented recovery-code flow
tradeoffs: Strong, free, and offline-capable, but requires users to have set up an authenticator app in advance and to have a backup/recovery-code plan for a lost device

best_for: A lower-friction MFA option to offer alongside TOTP, accepting the security tradeoff for broader adoption
avoid_when: High-value accounts (admin, financial) as the *only* MFA option — SIM-swap attacks specifically target SMS-based MFA
tradeoffs: Easier for users to set up than an authenticator app, but meaningfully weaker against a targeted attacker due to SIM-swap risk — offer, don't rely on exclusively

best_for: Every MFA implementation — generate a set of one-time-use backup codes at MFA setup time for when the user loses their second-factor device
avoid_when: Never skip — an MFA system with no recovery path turns a lost phone into a full account lockout, which becomes a costly support burden
tradeoffs: Give the user a way back into their account without weakening the MFA guarantee for an attacker who doesn't have the codes; each code is single-use and the set should be regenerable

best_for: Reducing signup friction and avoiding password-storage liability entirely for users willing to use an existing identity provider
avoid_when: A product where users specifically don't want a third party to know they use your service (some B2B/privacy-sensitive contexts) — offer email/password or magic link as an alternative
tradeoffs: Removes password storage/breach liability for those users and speeds up signup, but ties account recovery to the identity provider's own account-recovery flow if something goes wrong there

best_for: B2B SaaS selling to enterprise customers whose IT department requires centralized identity management via their own IdP (Okta, Azure AD, etc.)
avoid_when: Consumer products or small-team B2B — SSO/SAML implementation and per-customer IdP configuration is real ongoing operational overhead not justified below enterprise deal sizes
tradeoffs: Often a hard requirement to close enterprise deals and centralizes access control for the customer's IT team, but adds meaningful implementation and per-customer configuration overhead

option: Row-Level Security as the Authorization Layer
best_for: Supabase/Postgres apps where 'can this user see/modify this row' should be enforced at the database layer regardless of which code path queries it
avoid_when: Authorization logic that's too dynamic/computed to express as a SQL policy (e.g. depends on an external service call) — enforce that specific case in application code instead
tradeoffs: Database-enforced guarantee that survives even a buggy or malicious application-layer bypass attempt; pairs with DB015/DB030 for multi-tenant isolation specifically

best_for: Most apps with a handful of clear roles (admin, editor, viewer) where permissions map cleanly to a role, not to fine-grained per-resource rules
avoid_when: Permission logic that varies per resource instance in ways a fixed role can't express (e.g. 'owner of this specific document') — combine with ownership checks or move to ABAC
tradeoffs: Simple to reason about and audit ('what can an editor do') but can become a proliferation of near-duplicate roles if requirements get fine-grained enough

best_for: Complex authorization requirements where access depends on multiple attributes (user department, resource sensitivity, time of day, relationship to the resource) that RBAC's fixed roles can't express cleanly
avoid_when: Simple apps with a handful of roles — ABAC's flexibility is unneeded complexity there
tradeoffs: Handles genuinely complex, multi-factor authorization rules in one evaluated policy, at the cost of being harder to audit at a glance than a fixed role list

option: Claims Embedded in JWT (roles/permissions in the token)
best_for: Stateless APIs where you want to avoid a DB lookup on every request just to check the caller's role
avoid_when: Permissions that change frequently and need to take effect immediately — a claim baked into a still-valid JWT won't reflect a permission revoked mid-session until the token expires or is refreshed
tradeoffs: Avoids a database round-trip per request for authorization checks, but stale claims in a long-lived token are a real risk — keep access-token lifetimes short (pairs with DB033) if using this pattern

best_for: Service-to-service or third-party integrator access where there's no interactive user to log in — issue a scoped, revocable API key per consumer
avoid_when: Interactive end-user-facing auth — API keys are for machine/service consumers, not for a product's own user login
tradeoffs: Simple for consumers to use in server-to-server calls, but the key itself is a long-lived bearer credential — scope it minimally, support easy rotation/revocation, and never expose it client-side

best_for: Service-to-service auth where you want short-lived tokens instead of a long-lived static API key, and both services support OAuth
avoid_when: A quick internal integration where full OAuth client-credentials setup is disproportionate overhead — a scoped API key may be simpler
tradeoffs: Short-lived tokens reduce the blast radius of a leaked credential compared to a static API key, at the cost of more setup (token endpoint, client registration) than just issuing a key

best_for: Projects already on Supabase — get email/password, magic link, social OAuth, and RLS-integrated sessions without standing up separate auth infrastructure
avoid_when: A project needing an identity provider entirely decoupled from its database platform
tradeoffs: Auth state integrates directly with RLS policies (`auth.uid()` available in SQL), removing a whole category of 'keep app auth and DB authorization in sync' bugs — but ties you to Supabase's auth model specifically

best_for: Mobile-first apps already using Firebase, needing social login, anonymous auth, and custom claims with minimal setup
avoid_when: Apps needing deep Postgres-level authorization integration (RLS) — Firebase Auth pairs naturally with Firestore, not with a separate SQL database
tradeoffs: Extremely fast to set up for mobile with generous free tier, custom claims support role-like authorization, but less naturally integrated if your data layer is a SQL database rather than Firestore

best_for: Next.js (or similar) apps wanting a self-hosted, DB-agnostic auth library supporting many OAuth providers plus credentials/email, without a third-party managed service dependency
avoid_when: Teams wanting a fully managed, zero-maintenance identity provider — this is a library you host and configure yourself, not a hosted service
tradeoffs: Full control and no per-user pricing since you own the infrastructure, but you're responsible for its configuration, session storage adapter, and security updates yourself

best_for: Teams wanting a fully managed auth experience (hosted login UI, MFA, SSO, user management dashboard) without building any of it, especially pre-product-market-fit when speed matters most
avoid_when: Cost-sensitive projects at scale — these platforms typically price per monthly active user, which can become a meaningful line item as the user base grows
tradeoffs: Fastest path to production-grade auth (MFA, SSO, breach detection, compliance features) built in, at the cost of per-MAU pricing and a dependency on a third party for your login flow's uptime
